data.title: Nebius Stock Takes A Dive: What's Behind The Move?
data.description:
Nebius Group N.V. (NASDAQ:NBIS) shares are trading lower on Tuesday as software stocks broadly retreat, pressured by a wave of market weakness following President Donald Trump's latest tariff announcement targeting several European nations.

  • Nebius Group stock is showing notable weakness. Why is NBIS stock falling?

Trump's Tariff Announcement And Continued Greenland Push

In a social‑media post, Trump said that beginning Feb. 1, Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, the U.K., the Netherlands and Finland will face a 10% tariff on all goods shipped to the United States.

Trump's comments also revived his recent push to make Greenland a U.S. territory. In the same post, he said the tariff rate would jump to 25% on June 1, and remain in place until an agreement is reached for what he described as the "complete and total purchase of Greenland." He framed the plan as essential for global stability and U.S. national security, citing concerns involving China and Russia.

Nebius is a Dutch AI infrastructure company, headquartered in Amsterdam, Netherlands. Trump has said that the U.S. remains open to negotiations with Denmark and the other nations named in the tariff plan.

The Bearish Signals That Investors Can’t Ignore

Nebius is currently showing a bearish setup as it trades below key support levels. The RSI is neutral, indicating that there isn’t strong momentum in either direction. This suggests that traders should watch for a potential shift in momentum, as a move into overbought or oversold territory could signal more definitive price action.

MACD is currently below its signal line, indicating bearish pressure on Nebius’ stock, which suggests that the downward trend may continue unless there is a significant reversal in momentum.

Key support is at $94.50, and if this level is breached, it could signal further downside potential. Conversely, without a defined resistance level, traders may find it challenging to identify a clear target for a rebound.

Over the past 12 months, Nebius stock has gained 176.02%, which reflects a strong longer-term trend despite recent volatility. This impressive performance suggests that the stock has the potential for recovery, but traders need to be vigilant about current price levels and market conditions.

Currently, the stock is positioned at 70.0% of its 52-week range, indicating it’s closer to its highs than lows. This positioning suggests that while there is potential for further gains, the stock may also face increased selling pressure as it approaches previous highs.

NBIS Price Action: Nebius shares were down 5.33% at $102.94 at the time of publication on Tuesday, according to Benzinga Pro.

Image: Piotr Swat/Shutterstock

data.title: Crude Oil Gains 2%; D.R. Horton Earnings Top Views
data.description:
U.S. stocks traded lower midway through trading, with the Nasdaq Composite falling 1.5% on Tuesday as President Donald Trump’s threat to impose escalating tariffs on Europe over the Greenland dispute continued to roil global markets.

The Dow traded down 1.17% to 48,780.92 while the NASDAQ dipped 1.52% to 23,157.18. The S&P 500 also fell, dropping, 1.32% to 6,848.33.

Check This Out: How To Earn $500 A Month From Goldman Sachs Stock Ahead Of Q4 Earnings

Leading and Lagging Sectors

Energy shares gained by 0.3% on Tuesday.

In trading on Friday, information technology stocks dipped by 2%.

Top Headline

D.R. Horton, Inc. (NYSE:DHI) reported upbeat earnings for its fiscal 2026 first quarter.

Net income attributable to D.R. Horton totaled $594.8 million, or $2.03 per diluted share, for the three months ended Dec. 31, 2025, compared with $844.9 million, or $2.61 per diluted share, in the prior-year quarter. Earnings per share exceeded the $1.95 analyst estimate.

Revenue was $6.887 billion, down from $7.613 billion a year earlier. Sales also exceeded expectations, surpassing the $6.603 billion consensus estimate.

Equities Trading UP
           

  • Rapt Therapeutics Inc (NASDAQ:RAPT) shares shot up 64% to $57.52 after GSK announced it will acquire the company.
  • Shares of Corvus Pharmaceuticals Inc (NASDAQ:CRVS) got a boost, surging 107% to $16.66 after announcing results from cohort 4 of randomized, blinded, placebo-controlled Phase 1 clinical trial evaluating soquelitinib in patients with moderate to severe atopic dermatitis.
  • Immunitybio Inc (NASDAQ:IBRX) shares were also up, gaining 23% to $6.78 after the company announced that it recently held a Type B End-of-Phase meeting with the FDA regarding its supplemental Biologics License Application for ANKTIVA plus Bacillus Calmette-Guérin in BCG-unresponsive non-muscle invasive bladder cancer with papillary tumors.

Equities Trading DOWN

  • Bakkt Holdings Inc (NYSE:BKKT) shares dropped 19% to $17.42 after the company announced a $300 million ATM equity program.
  • Shares of NovaBay Pharmaceuticals Inc (NYSE:NBY) were down 45% to $8.07. Novabay Pharmaceuticals filed for offering of up to $100 million common stock.
  • Synlogic Inc (NASDAQ:SYBX) was down, falling 38% to $0.70 after the company received Nasdaq delisting notification.

Commodities

In commodity news, oil traded up 2% to $60.62 while gold traded up 3.6% at $4,759.30.

Silver traded up 7.1% to $94.845 on Tuesday, while copper fell 0.8% to $5.7845.

Euro zone

European shares were lower today. The eurozone's STOXX 600 fell 0.92%, while Spain's IBEX 35 Index fell 1.35%. London's FTSE 100 dipped 0.97%, Germany's DAX fell 1.14% and France's CAC 40 slipped 0.86% during the session.

Asia Pacific Markets

Asian markets closed mostly lower on Tuesday, with Japan's Nikkei falling 1.11%, Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index declining 0.29%, China's Shanghai Composite falling 0.01% and India's BSE Sensex falling 1.28%.

Economics

The ADP reported that US private employers added an average of 8,000 jobs per week during the four weeks ending Dec. 27, compared to an average gain of 11,250 in the prior period.

Photo via Shutterstock

data.title: Bitcoin Stuck At $90,000 For 2 Months—Where Did All The Volatility Go?
data.description:
Bitcoin (CRYPTO: BTC) has been trading in a range for two months, with traders left wondering in what direction the next impulse move will come.

Is BTC Repeating A 2022 Pattern?

Cycle analyst Cryptollica challenges the bear market narrative by looking beyond crypto-native history and comparing Bitcoin to long-term macro assets.

Drawing on Mark Twain's idea that history "rhymes," the analysis highlights a striking fractal similarity between Bitcoin's price evolution since 2008 and Japan's Nikkei 225 during its 1950–1990 economic expansion.

Both assets appear to follow a four-cycle structure, with the possibility of entering a fifth, hyper-growth phase.

According to Cryptollica, Bitcoin is currently positioned at a technical level like where the Nikkei stood in the mid-1980s, just before a powerful parabolic run.

While critics point to Bitcoin's break below the 50-day moving average as a classic bear signal, the analysis argues this is a trap rather than a top, suggesting this cycle is structurally different from past ones.

Supercycle Or Bear Market?

Analyst Garrett adds that comparing today's Bitcoin market to 2022 is misleading because it ignores critical macro and structural differences.

In 2022, Bitcoin peaked amid soaring inflation, aggressive rate hikes, tightening liquidity, and a clear risk-off environment, conditions that drove distribution and a prolonged bear market.

By contrast, today's setup features stabilization and absorption across key zones, particularly between $80,850 and $62,000, improving the bullish risk–reward profile.

A true 2022-style bear market, Garrett argues, would require a fresh inflation shock, renewed aggressive monetary tightening, and a sustained breakdown below major support levels, none of which are currently evident.

A Structural Shift In The Investor Base

Perhaps the most important difference is who owns Bitcoin today. The 2020–2022 cycle was dominated by retail speculation and high leverage.

Since 2023, spot Bitcoin ETFs have brought in long-term institutional holders who lock up supply, dampen volatility, and provide steadier demand.

This shift has fundamentally changed Bitcoin's volatility regime and market behavior, marking a structural inflection point. As a result, analysts argue that direct comparisons to the 2022 crypto-native bear market are analytically flawed.

Whether Bitcoin ultimately enters a super cycle or simply continues a slower expansion, the conditions driving price today are not the same ones that defined the last major downturn.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: The Buffett Indicator Signals Elevated Risk As Ratio Hits 222 Percent
data.description:
Investors are closely watching a classic market valuation metric: the Buffett Indicator. This ratio, which compares the total market capitalization of U.S. equities to the country's gross domestic product, recently climbed to 222 percent. Historically, readings above 200 percent have often preceded market corrections, prompting analysts and investors to consider the potential risks for stocks in the near term.

Understanding the Buffett Indicator

The Buffett Indicator is named after billionaire investor Warren Buffett, who has frequently referenced it as a gauge of overall market valuation. It is calculated by dividing the total market capitalization of U.S. stocks by the nation's GDP. A ratio above 100 percent indicates that the market is valued higher than the annual economic output, while a ratio below 100 percent suggests a more conservative valuation.

A reading of 222 percent signals that U.S. equities are more than double the size of the economy. Historically, such elevated levels have coincided with periods of market overvaluation, including the dot-com bubble in the late 1990s and the pre-2008 financial crisis period. While not a timing tool, the Buffett Indicator serves as a warning that valuations may be stretched relative to economic fundamentals.

Historical Context

Looking at past cycles, the Buffett Indicator has demonstrated a strong correlation with market peaks. In 1999, the ratio exceeded 150 percent before the technology-heavy Nasdaq Composite experienced a severe correction. In 2007, the ratio approached similar elevated levels ahead of the financial crisis, when the S&P 500 (NYSE:SPY) fell significantly over the following year.

The current reading of 222 percent is among the highest in modern history, rivaling the extreme levels seen during prior speculative bubbles. While market dynamics have evolved particularly with the rise of mega-cap technology stocks and unprecedented monetary policy—valuation metrics at these extremes often indicate heightened risk.

Implications for Investors

A Buffett Indicator reading this high suggests caution, especially for investors heavily concentrated in growth-oriented sectors. Mega-cap stocks, including Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL), Microsoft Corporation (NASDAQ:MSFT), and Amazon.com Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN), have seen valuations surge even as broader economic growth has moderated. Elevated ratios may limit upside potential and increase vulnerability to corrections if market sentiment shifts.

However, it is important to note that elevated readings do not guarantee an immediate market downturn. Factors such as low interest rates, strong corporate earnings, and investor optimism can sustain high valuations for extended periods. Nevertheless, investors should consider managing risk, diversifying portfolios, and evaluating exposure to highly valued sectors.

Why the Ratio Is So High

Several factors contribute to the current elevated Buffett Indicator. Strong earnings growth among large-cap technology companies has significantly increased market capitalization. Simultaneously, GDP growth has moderated, partly due to slower post-pandemic recovery in certain sectors. Low interest rates and quantitative easing measures have also encouraged higher equity valuations relative to economic output.

This combination—high market capitalization, moderate economic growth, and abundant liquidity—creates conditions where the Buffett Indicator signals caution. It does not necessarily indicate an imminent crash but highlights the potential for increased volatility.

Market Outlook

Analysts suggest that investors should monitor complementary indicators alongside the Buffett Indicator, such as price-to-earnings ratios, margin debt levels, and investor sentiment surveys. While the ratio is a long-term valuation gauge, these additional metrics can provide context for timing risk management decisions.

Historically, markets can remain elevated for extended periods. For example, during the late 1990s dot-com era, the Buffett Indicator remained high for more than a year before the eventual correction. Similarly, elevated ratios were present in 2021-2022 without immediate market collapse, reflecting the complex interplay of liquidity, earnings, and investor behavior.

For long-term investors, the indicator serves as a reminder to remain disciplined, consider rebalancing exposure, and focus on fundamentals. For traders, it may highlight areas where volatility could increase if sentiment shifts or macroeconomic shocks occur.

What Investors Should Know

The Buffett Indicator at 222 percent serves as a cautionary signal for equity investors. While it does not predict the exact timing of market corrections, it highlights the potential for elevated risk relative to economic fundamentals. Investors should incorporate this metric into a broader analysis, considering macroeconomic trends, corporate earnings, and valuation multiples across sectors.

As markets continue to evolve, the Buffett Indicator remains a useful tool to gauge overall market health, providing insight into whether equity prices are aligned with the underlying economy or extended beyond sustainable levels.

Benzinga Disclaimer: This article is from an unpaid external contributor. It does not represent Benzinga’s reporting and has not been edited for content or accuracy.

data.title: Nvidia's Gain, Your Loss: Micron Confirms 100% Sell-Through to AI Leaders
data.description:
Micron Technology , Inc. (NASDAQ:MU) Executive VP Manish Bhatia confirmed last Friday that the AI infrastructure boom has triggered an “unprecedented” shortage of memory chips, as production capacity is diverted away from everyday consumer goods to satisfy AI titans. 

The meteoric rise of AI is creating a zero-sum game for the global electronics market. 

  • MU stock is climbing. See the chart and price action here.

‘Unprecedented' Shortage

The shift to artificial intelligence is so profound that Micron is effectively turning its back on the rest of the electronics market. 

To prioritize enterprise giants like Nvidia Corp. (NASDAQ:NVDA) and Microsoft Corp. (NASDAQ:MSFT), Micron announced in December it will discontinue its popular Crucial-branded consumer memory business. 

The demand from AI hyperscalers is so great that it is "consuming so much of the available capacity across the industry that it's leaving a tremendous shortage for the conventional side of the industry, for phones or PCs," Bhatia said, per Bloomberg. 

Counterpoint Research predicts a 2.1% drop in global smartphone shipments this year as prices soar due to the high-end memory shortage. 

Dell Technologies, Inc. (NYSE:DELL) and other PC manufacturers have warned that the scarcity of standard DRAM—the working memory of computers—will lead to higher prices and lower availability.

Micron's $100 Billion Domestic Move

Micron officially broke ground Friday on its $100 billion mega-site in New York in order to expand domestic production.  

U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick attended the ground-breaking and highlighted the project as a cornerstone of national security. 

The production site will be the largest semiconductor facility in the U.S. and is expected to generate 50,000 jobs, Micron CEO Sanjay Mehrotra told CNBC on Tuesday. 

The site will eventually house four massive fabs, aiming to bring 40% of Micron's DRAM production to U.S. soil, supported by a $6.2 billion CHIPS Act award and a 35% investment tax credit.

Accelerating the Timeline

While the New York site won’t produce wafers until 2030, Micron is moving aggressively elsewhere to plug the immediate gap:

On Saturday, Micron announced a $1.8 billion purchase of an existing plant in Taiwan to fast-track DRAM output by 2027.

Ultimately, the memory required for autonomous robots and AI accelerators has become Micron's priority.  

The conventional side of the tech world—and consumers' wallets—will bear the cost until more manufacturing comes online. 

Photo: Shutterstock
